title: Whats The Deal With This Evangelion
display_title: Whats The Deal With This Evangelion
wikitext:
[]: "...What's The Deal With This Evangelion?"             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Mon Apr 09, 2007 2:44 pm As I sometimes do when I'm playing online poker, my mind wanders. So I click on EMF... Bleh, dead. I click on ANF - Eva section, and started reading (haven't started posting on there much yet). I came upon THIS thread which lead me to THIS thread and the idea peaked my interest.... "Why the big fuss and uber-analyzation of what amounts to just a 26 episode + movie anime show?" I think I know exactly why and I want to see if everyone here agrees with me: While it might be the complex plot and labrynthine narrative that keeps us coming back to figure out NGE's many intricacies, what ultimately hooks you is the bond you develope with the characters. We all know that NGE was created out of Anno's depression, so much of it is merely a symbolism for his battle with that affliction. The characters are an extention of that. However, what makes these characters unique is that we get such an in depth look at their personalities, we realize that, in many ways, they're just like us. Is there any one here who can say they can't relate to anyone in NGE? Whether it's Shinji's shy, introverted personality, or Asuka's brash, extroverted personality, these two represent the extremes of the human psychology. And most people's personalities can be (largely) made up of a certain mix of these two characteristics. It's the idea that they're depicted in such a truthful way that we're able to relate to them so well. By the end, at the very least we care deeply what happens to them. At the most, the characters become mirrors to our own personal psyches and personalities - just like they no doubt were to Anno's. That's why the ending has so much impact. That's why we feel like we've been through as much of a journey as the characters have by the end. Everyone on here can say that "no, it's just the inner workings of NGE as a narrative and complex cinematic work that blah blah"... Face it, without that emotional connection to the characters, there's really no desire to dig any deeper. A poster in the other thread made a good point: "Why no big fuss over other animes with equally strong philosophical/psychological symbolism and messages and complex narratives"? It's because those series don't do nearly the job that NGE does making the viewer empathize and become one with the characters' struggles. IMO, this is the ultimate testament to NGE's genius. Great works of art are often born out of personal struggle. Because within those struggle the artist is better able to connect with people, and say something meaningful. NGE is that work in the world of anime, IMO. So, for a shortened summary of my answer: There's a sense of real connection between the viewer and the characters in NGE that creates an emotional attachment. This initial attachment creates interest, and when the series ends, this attachment leads to a real interest in the inner workings of the series itself. That interest often lingers because one then realizes just how complex a series it is. And the uber-analyzation begins.

[]: Re: "...What's The Deal With This Evangelion?"             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Mon Apr 09, 2007 3:04 pm Eva Yojimbo wrote: There's a sense of real connection between the viewer and the and the characters in NGE that creates an emotional attachment.

[]: Re: &quot;...What's The Deal With This Evangelion?&q             Quote Post by Opteron-O3 » Mon Apr 09, 2007 3:42 pm Eva Yojimbo wrote: By the end, at the very least we care deeply what happens to them. At the most, the characters become mirrors to our own personal psyches and personalities - just like they no doubt were to Anno's. That's why the ending has so much impact. That's why we feel like we've been through as much of a journey as the characters have by the end. Everyone on here can say that "no, it's just the inner workings of NGE as a narrative and complex cinematic work that blah blah"... Face it, without that emotional connection to the characters, there's really no desire to dig any deeper. A poster in the other thread made a good point: "Why no big fuss over other animes with equally strong philosophical/psychological symbolism and messages and complex narratives"? It's because those series don't do nearly the job that NGE does making the viewer empathize and become one with the characters' struggles.

[]:             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Mon Apr 09, 2007 3:56 pm As I'm sure I've said before -- I believe what makes NGE "sticky" for some people is that the ones who do are the ones who are prepared to own some of the problems suffered by the characters. If you can't admit the likeness -- or are luckily untroubled by the same sort of issues -- then it won't be any more than a show about a cast of misfits and a 2001-style conclusion.

[]:             Quote Post by Opteron-O3 » Mon Apr 09, 2007 3:58 pm Mr. Tines wrote: As I'm sure I've said before -- I believe what makes NGE "sticky" for some people is that the ones who do are the ones who are prepared to own some of the problems suffered by the characters. If you can't admit the likeness -- or are luckily untroubled by the same sort of issues -- then it won't be any more than a show about a cast of misfits and a 2001-style conclusion.

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Mon Apr 09, 2007 4:05 pm Mr. Tines wrote: As I'm sure I've said before -- I believe what makes NGE "sticky" for some people is that the ones who do are the ones who are prepared to own some of the problems suffered by the characters. If you can't admit the likeness -- or are luckily untroubled by the same sort of issues -- then it won't be any more than a show about a cast of misfits and a 2001-style conclusion.

[]:             Quote Post by Opteron-O3 » Mon Apr 09, 2007 4:10 pm Eva Yojimbo wrote: Mr. Tines wrote: As I'm sure I've said before -- I believe what makes NGE "sticky" for some people is that the ones who do are the ones who are prepared to own some of the problems suffered by the characters. If you can't admit the likeness -- or are luckily untroubled by the same sort of issues -- then it won't be any more than a show about a cast of misfits and a 2001-style conclusion.

[]:             Quote Post by Executor » Mon Apr 09, 2007 7:24 pm The thing I dont like so much about Evangelion is the fact that pretty much none of the charaters really connect to me. (My opnion, but you knew that.) Dont get me wrong, I like the Anime, or I wouldent post here.

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Tue Apr 10, 2007 6:53 am Executor wrote: The thing I dont like so much about Evangelion is the fact that pretty much none of the charaters really connect to me.

[]:             Quote Post by Morzella » Tue Apr 10, 2007 1:01 pm What would your personality be that you can't relate to anyone in NGE?

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Tue Apr 10, 2007 1:18 pm Morzella wrote: Well adjusted and socialy capaebul?

[]:             Quote Post by Morzella » Tue Apr 10, 2007 1:38 pm I agree completly. The idea of being "normal" is an idealised falsehood. As the chacters of Eva chactrerise the fears and hopes real people hold so well the only way to have nothing in common with them would be to meet this perfect idea. I probaly should have added a /sarcasm. Well I live and learn.

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Tue Apr 10, 2007 1:50 pm Morzella wrote: I agree completly. The idea of being "normal" is an idealised falsehood. As the chacters of Eva chactrerise the fears and hopes real people hold so well the only way to have nothing in common with them would be to meet this perfect idea. I probaly should have added a /sarcasm. Well I live and learn.

[]:             Quote Post by Opteron-O3 » Tue Apr 10, 2007 2:22 pm Eva Yojimbo wrote: Maybe some pretend like these fears don't exist, and they build up walls (AT Fields?) to protect themselves, but that's just an illusion. Perhaps the illusion becomes so strong that people actually begin to believe it's true.

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Tue Apr 10, 2007 3:20 pm Opteron-O3 wrote: "...At field is the light of your soul, the sacred territory which no one may intrude... inclosed is..." I know it's probably not word for word, tried to remember it off the top of my head. I need to look it up. Anyways... sounds like an illusion to me ;) "...we are all fundamentally alone..." don't rmeember who says it but it's something like that.

[]:             Quote Post by Morzella » Tue Apr 10, 2007 6:08 pm It's like folding paper: Start with a piece of paper as 1, fold to get 2, fold again to get 4, again to 8, to 16, to 32, to 64, and so on.

[]:             Quote Post by TriLink » Tue Apr 10, 2007 7:03 pm Eva Yojimbo wrote: "Why no big fuss over other animes with equally strong philosophical/psychological symbolism and messages and complex narratives"? It's because those series don't do nearly the job that NGE does making the viewer empathize and become one with the characters' struggles.

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Tue Apr 10, 2007 7:09 pm Morzella wrote: Just a bit of trivia but it's actully only possibul to fold any given peice of paper about eight times.

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Tue Apr 10, 2007 7:11 pm TriLink12 wrote: @BTW- I move that this thread be placed in the Discussion Forum so it will garner the truely thoughtful posts it deserves.

[]:             Quote Post by THE Hal E. Burton 9000 » Tue Apr 10, 2007 10:45 pm The biggest "deal" with Eva IMO is the simple, yet profound, fact is that the direction of the show did not ultimately follow and end like the episodic narratives in nearly all other films, novels, TV shows, etc. And not to mention the fact that the character-driven plot line was something that almost everyone could find something to relate to, in spite of the absolutely graceless ending, is what has drawn people, including myself and most on this forum, to Evangelion like wolves to red meat.
